data.title: IonQ: Remaining Bullish Even After Recent Pullback
data.description:
IonQ (IONQ) is one of the several hotly debated quantum computing names in the market, with the stock volatility remaining high. IONQ provides investors with a pure-play opportunity to gain direct exposure to the emerging quantum trend, a highly speculative part of the market that has gained material traction over the past year.

My last article on IONQ may have topped the stock price, but I continue to believe there is long-term value in this name. The company has released a slew of positive updates over the past several months, and their November earnings report was another positive update.

As seen in the chart above, volatility should be expected with this investment. With the company still very early in its commercialization journey, the stock often trades on news headlines and progress towards its technological advancements rather than on pure fundamentals.

Over the long term, I do believe that financials will become increasingly important; however, that is not the focus now or over the next several quarters. Investors should be looking at this investment as a speculative and highly volatile asset that comes with high risks.

That being said, IONQ has provided numerous key updates in recent months, highlighted by a strong earnings report. The company's technology has a unique competitive advantage, and its early success building out quantum computing, networking, and security features is starting to gain more traction across academics and users.

While volatility will more than likely continue for the foreseeable future, this is a name where long-term investors should look to buy on weakness.

Financial and Technology Updates

IONQ hosted an analyst day a few months ago that included a detailed review of its technological structure and advantages. I discussed this a bit more in my prior article.

The most important message was clear. The future of quantum computing will inherently be networked, with quantum processors not likely to work in isolation. There will need to be quantum links in order to efficiently scale performance and effectively distribute the workloads. IONQ's networking capabilities are starting to become unparalleled in the market, making this competitive advantage even more important. By taking ownership across both the computing and networking layers, IONQ can integrate quantum memory and photonic interconnects that will ultimately improve scalability.

IONQ reported its Q3 earnings in early November, and the financial results were better than expected, albeit on very small revenue figures. Revenue during the quarter was $39.9 million, which was above estimates for ~$27.0 million. Yes, this is a "big" beat, but this is all on very small dollar amounts relative to the company's near-$18 billion market cap. Again, the fundamentals of revenue and profit are almost meaningless at this point in the company's growth stage.

The trajectory of revenue will be important, as this needs to keep accelerating. But I believe the two bigger financial metrics to follow are cash burn and liquidity.

On cash burn, adjusted EBITDA loss during the quarter was nearly $49 million, bringing the year-to-date total to over $119 million. This also compares to FCF burn of almost $220 million so far this year. Staggering amounts of losses relative to the $68 million of revenue generated so far this year, but this is exactly what the company is going after. Massive amounts of up-front investments to build out its technology stack and prepare for the eventual product commercialization. This will come at a high cost.

When it comes to liquidity, IONQ raised $2 billion via an equity offering in October, which brought its total pro-forma cash to ~$3.5 billion. Although the company does not have any debt, there is ~$1.8 billion of warrant liabilities on the balance sheet, so investors should expect some form of share dilution at some point in the future. Given the near $18 billion valuation on the stock, it is smart for the company to capitalize on its equity price.

As mentioned earlier, the non-financial updates will be a much better indicator of stock action, and this past quarter provided several positive updates, as seen within the earnings press release.

Management also provided several key business updates on the earnings call.

During the quarter, we announced that our #AQ 64 Tempo system was up and running three months ahead of schedule, fulfilling our technical goal for the year. We closed our acquisition of Oxford Ionics and raised $1 billion at a 25% premium to the previous day's closing price. We created IonQ Federal and appointed a leadership team consisting of senior security cleared personnel, including Executive Chairman, Robert Cardillo, former Director of the National Geospatial Intelligence Agency for President Trump.

In my opinion, the update on the #AQ 64 Tempo system three months ahead of schedule was the most significant progress. This not only demonstrates that the company's quantum methods are capable of these advancements, but also that the progress is happening at an accelerated pace.

In addition to the Oxford Ionics acquisition mentioned above, IONQ also acquired Skyloom Global shortly after earnings, giving them access to high performance optical communications infrastructure.

Are these announcements all game changing events? No. But I believe that in totality, these are all positive indicators of underlying operational successes over the past few months. One of the keys to my investment thesis is ongoing technological advancements, expanded partnerships and customer announcements, and maintaining comfortable liquidity balances.

Valuation

As mentioned earlier, the stock is not currently being valued on fundamentals. As with many early-stage disruptive technology companies, valuations is often highly disconnected from fundamentals. Rather, investors will look at the multi-year growth potential, years before material revenue inflection, and ignore the near-term valuation levels.

I believe this will continue to be the case for IONQ and many other quantum names for the foreseeable future.

The stock is currently trading at over 160x forward revenue. And with the company guiding to full-year revenue of $106-110 million, there is a clear disconnect with its near $18 billion valuation.

Even if revenue were to 4x over the next year, the stock would still be trading over 40x revenue, a multiple difficult to accept for almost any other stock in the market.

But this goes to my earlier points. Fundamentals become almost irrelevant right now. Investors focus should only be on the technological advancements, which IONQ has demonstrated their ability to progress ahead of time. I believe ongoing positive updates like this past quarter can help push the stock higher, even with valuation already being at nosebleed levels.

Risks

One of the biggest risks to my bullish thesis is if IONQ's technology and/or commercialization strategy ends up failing. If this were to happen, the stock could become a $0. Without the right technology and product in place, success would become nearly impossible. Sure, revenue is expected to be $106-110 million, which is a strong start to commercialization, but much more revenue will be needed to justify the current valuation.

Additionally, competition is very fierce and likely to intensify over the coming years. With many pure-play quantum companies attacking the market in different ways, it becomes very challenging to know who the winner will be. Add on the competitive threats from the likes of IBM and Google, who have much larger technological and financial resources at hand, and this market is poised to be competitive for a long time.

Final Thoughts

Timing IONQ's stock is nearly impossible given the amount of volatility and sentiment swings. However, the recent pullback in shares seems to be more reflective of a broader "risk-off" market environment. One only needs to look at some of the AI exposed names to see this rotation occurring in the market.

IONQ has developed a niche competitive advantage, and the recent quarterly update and subsequent announcements have been positive. The company has also strengthened its liquidity position, giving them significant flexibility to invest organically as well as through acquisitions.

Ultimately, success here is not guaranteed. However, I believe IONQ remains the best positioned pure-play quantum name and long-term investors should look to take advantage of periods of weakness.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Not Where I Want It To Be Just Yet
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Occidental Petroleum (OXY) caught a lot of investors' eyes as Warren Buffett opened a position back in 2022. Since then the share price has returned to a similar price level after initially rising sharply to $75 per share before beginning a steady fall.

On face value the stock doesn’t look cheap, and looking at the recent and historical operating performance, I am not impressed enough to make it a buy yet. I find the upside limited and that there still exists some downside risk if the investments in the Barnett Shale don’t pan out. Rating OXY a hold as I begin coverage.

Overview of Operations - Segment Performance

OXY is a rather interesting company to follow, not because the oracle of Omaha, Warren Buffett, bought the stock in 2022; he still owns a 26.9% stake, and as of 2025, is now set to buy out the Chemical segment from the company as well and bring that to Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), with the transaction expected to close in Q4 2025. No, the reason I find OXY interesting is because of its vertical integration, where it owns and operates a pretty significant upstream segment but then also has midstream assets and a marketing segment too which handles all of the produced volumes, both for selling and for transporting.

The key issue the company has been struggling with is the margins. As of the last quarter, operating margins came in at 14.8%. This marks another series of declines from the highs in 2022 at 37.3%. Of course, those who follow the energy sector will know that during 2022 both oil and gas prices surged, so it’s no wonder that year is an anomaly. In today's article I’ll try and highlight a little more of the actual core performance of the company and what direction it seems to be heading in right now. Reporting wise there are three segments, and soon to be just two, as the Chemicals get divested before the year ends. The remaining are Oil & Gas, followed by Midstream & Marketing. The first is by far the most important on the consolidated financial results.

The business model itself is quite straightforward; the Oil & Gas segment represents the upstream assets, and the second segment is sort of built around this. Because of this approach, it tends to eliminate certain third-party fees and drive better profitability, and importantly, OXY can ensure its oil and gas have somewhere to go and don't get stuck in a bottleneck. The added advantage is also that the OXY has CO₂ pipelines that send this back to the oil fields, specifically more mature fields, to perform something referred to as “Enhanced Oil Recovery”, in essence prolonging the asset life and getting more oil. Since 2018, OXY has increased its oil production by about 147%, going from 256 MMBL to 643 MMBL.

Oil & Gas

Oil is by far the biggest source of revenue for the company. It has and continues to hover around the 80% mark of all revenues generated going back to 2018. What has shifted slightly is how natural gas has, in periods, contributed with serious revenue and, in turn, earnings boosts. This refers back to my earlier comment on how during 2022 the price for this gas soared to $8.81 MBtu for a period, before falling down to a low of $1.49 MBtu in 2024.

The reason for OXY being able to increase oil production volumes by so much has been primarily through acquisitions. The first was in 2019 when it outbid Chevron (CVX) for Anadarko for $38 billion. The second and more recent would be CrownRock, which closed last year, valued at $12 billion. The most appealing part about the second being the assets OXY bought. CrownRock had a lot of operating area in a region called Barnett Shale in the western part of Texas. This region started out as a dry-gas play but has transformed into a now oil-rich play, and OXY now owns prime “real estate” for this.

In an investor presentation, the company lays out how the Barnett Shale offers lower cost and higher production than many other regions. The deep well types here are more expensive to operative, but it's offset by the sheer quantity that is drilled.

One of the key attributes of oil production is the byproduct that is NGL (Natural Gas Liquids). This is produced when also drilling for oil, and because of the rising oil production in the Permian, it’s become an increasingly important product for companies like OXY as well. As of the last report, 10.3% of the revenues came from this, and it has remained steady at this level, but perhaps most important is how, in times of lower oil prices, it has been able to pick up some of the “slack”.

Above is a table showing the liquids yield ratio based on oil production, combining both US and International sales. Arguably, I think sales is better to look at as opposed to the production levels since this is the monetized value as opposed to just the produced figure.

Because OXY has been able to drive more NGL yield as a % of total production, it has meant revenues were $557 million in Q3 2025, up from $240 million in 2019. At the same yield, last quarter's NGL revenue contribution would have been closer to $350 - $400 million instead.

Midstream & Marketing

Moving over to the second segment is the Midstream and Marketing. This is what enables the vertical integration of OXY by handling the output from its upstream assets. The asset base here consists of processing plants, pipelines, gathering and compression facilities. According to the last 2024 annual report, OXY owned 14,371 miles of pipelines and 15.05 Bcf/d in capacity across all assets.

The actual accounting practice for this segment is a little harder to disaggregate. The company has changed practice quite a few times over the past few years in what figures it reports and doesn't, as I’ve been digging through the past 6 to 7 years. In any case, what is clear is that revenues have shown a pretty poor performance overall, going from a high in Q3 2022 of $991 million to now below $400 million.

What has been a large contributing factor here is the equity investments, namely two: Western Midstream (WES) and Dolphin Energy (DEL). OXY owns a 49% stake in the company; the latest 10Q or 10K doesn't state a specific figure. The ownership came as a result of buying Anadarko a few years ago. In early 2024, OXY was, according to some, seeking to divest. Since then shares have remained largely flat, but yields are 9.43% on an FWD basis currently.

Chemical - Getting Divested to Berkshire Hathaway

In the all-cash deal of $9.7 billion for the third segment, OXY will be able to get a nice and much-needed cash influx for the balance sheet. With $20.8 billion in long-term debt and $1.6 billion due within the next 12 months, OXY drastically improves its financial position like this. The company hasn’t stated what exactly the proceeds would be going towards, but given the state of the balance sheet and the recent acquisitions the company has been doing, I have to think the focus lies more on the Barnett Shale region than on buying another company with the proceeds.

The segment itself makes chlorine, caustic soda, and PVC and works with a number of end-markets. Despite being a large portion of the total company-wide revenues, growth has been quite stale and even declining slightly between Q3 2018 and the most recent report. Personally, I think the decision is the right one. The segment has been a solid source of net income, $373 million in Q3 2023. More recent figures are hard to find, again going back to my critique about OXY seemingly changing accounting practice from year to year.

Carbon Capture - A Future Cash Cow?

One project that OXY has been quite vocal about is their carbon capture business, set to capture 500,000 tonnes per year once in full production. The $1.3 billion Texan facility is set to be in full production before 2027, it seems, if all goes well.

The idea is great in theory: a new revenue stream. As the leaning off non-renewable energy sources isn't coming fast enough it means that the next best thing is capturing and storing the carbon emissions instead.

When I’m reading some of the forecasts for the direct-air-capture market, I feel there is a lot of hype and not a lot of economic reality. One report suggests it being a trillion dollar industry by 2034, growing from $260 billion in 2026. I highly doubt that because if so, OXY and all other energy companies would be pouring a lot more capital into the space than they’re currently doing. A $1.3 billion facility won't make a dent. Since the DAC credit market is highly illiquid, as prices are set up in person more or less, it makes for a very fragmented outlook. The closest figures we have might be from tech-companies carbon credits, which can range from $170 - $500 per tonne. At full-scale and full revenues, that would add $250 million in revenues for OXY. It makes more in 2 quarters of dividends from its investments in WES and DEL.

Because the market is still very new for this, I think price projections need to be taken with a teaspoon of salt and market size predictions with a large tablespoon instead.

Hard to Reach Any Form of Value Here

The main issue I have with OXY as an investment is purely about the value proposition. I don’t find a sufficient amount of it here to make it a buy. By no means is the business bad, which makes a sell rating seem excessive. But given the valuation and some of the disappointing results for both operating margin and the Midstream and Marketing segment, I think a hold is the most fitting here. Because OXY is an upstream company, predominantly financial results are going to vary wildly from year to year, as I’ve discussed throughout the article.

On a FWD P/E non-GAAP basis, it looks expensive at 18.25x, and 19.82x using GAAP estimates. Considering the sector median is 13.75 and 14.6, respectively.

On top of this the company does not have a strong allocation plan yet, which includes an appealing dividend or buybacks. OXY does pay a dividend, make no mistake, but at $1.563 billion paid over the past 12 months it's not that much when the market cap is hovering around the $40 billion mark, with a FWD yield of 2.33% right now. Considering the 3.2% annual growth rate and just 4 consecutive years of raising, I don't have a lot of faith this can be a long-term compounder unless it seriously gets its operating margins in order.

The payout ratio in relation to the FCF has changed drastically over the past few years. Now OXY generates far more in OCF, largely because of depreciation accumulating and doubling from $4.403 billion in 2017 to now $8.225 billion TTM. During the same period, the total asset value has grown from $42 billion to $83 billion. Given OXY is selling off its chemical segment, it will mean that the OCF will decline slightly, seeing as the segment did contribute a fair bit to overall operational income.

Where OXY does look a lot cheaper is the EV/EBITDA metric, at 5.36x FWD basis. Much of the reason the EBITDA has risen so much over the past few years seems tied to rising depreciation from both acquisition and asset base growth. It is a better metric to look at for OXY as opposed to things like P/S because of price volatility in crude oil and gas. Falling below $60 per barrel, now the outlook for oil is mixed, which does put pressure on future EBITDA and cash flows for OXY.

Potential Catalyst

I'm rating the stock a hold, as I've made clear now. What could swing the pendulum either way, so to speak, involves both of the companies own internal margins. If margins continue trending down and volumes do too, along with lower overall oil prices. The EIA has slightly raised their earlier guidance for next year's prices, which should hopefully improve the earnings for OXY a bit.

What could further spark an increase in oil might be fear. By this I am referring to the increasing amount of strikes on oil vessels, seizing oil tankers. This is creating uncertainty in the oil market, and that usually leads to higher prices.

Since the earnings picture for OXY is heavily dependent on the price of oil, the direction of that commodity sort of decides the stock price as well. Personally, I think there is a potential for a surge in oil prices next year given the current climate. With that said, I think I would be more prone to upgrading my rating rather than downgrading.

The Full Picture

After now discussing OXY, I think the picture is a little like this: at the core it has a solid crude oil upstream asset base but has been struggling with consistent margins. In 2020 it cut its dividend but has been building it back up without overleveraging in terms of payout as a % to the FCF generated. It is a bonus being vertically integrated, referring to the midstream segment; it hasn't necessarily produced the returns I’d like or would have hoped to see. Because there are equity investments located in that segment, those currently seem to drive the vast majority of earnings there, indicating a relatively lackluster segment performance overall.

At the end of the day, the company has a vast exposure to the Permian and other basins, and the acquisitions it has made through the years has helped solidify this. The current value proposal is lacking, and I find the stock to be better rated as a hold.

data.title: EEM: Few Investors Will Benefit From This Unknown Cycle That Has Occurred Since 2003
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM). This article is the continuation of my initial coverage article published on December 5, 2024, also with a buy recommendation. My intention with this article is to analyze the performance of the investment thesis in 2025 and project the prospects for 2026.

Context

At the end of 2024, I wanted to generate more value for the reader of Seeking Alpha, and I thought I would share my most promising idea for 2025: investing in emerging markets. Today, at the end of 2025, I consider that the idea was successful, as the EEM ETF rose 28% since then, while the S&P 500 rose only 13.3%.

For 2026 I would like to revisit this thesis and explain why I still believe in the potential of investment. To do this, I will write a straight-to-the-point article with four strong arguments for investors to invest in ETF EEM, but before presenting these arguments, let's learn a little more about ETF EEM.

iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF

The EEM ETF is managed by BlackRock, has $20.9 billion AUM, and has a 0.72% expense ratio. As we can see below, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing holds 11.39% participation, but it is interesting to note that from the eighth main holding onwards, the participation of companies is already less than 1%; that is, there is a lot of diversification since there are 1262 holdings.

Another attraction, in my opinion, is the exposure that the main holding companies give to artificial intelligence. It is interesting to note that we have pairs of this ETF, such as the SPDR® Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF, which has an expense ratio of 0.07%, and the Templeton Emerging Markets Fund Inc. which has an expense ratio of 1.34%. It is curious that the EMF ETF has the highest expense ratio, but it also has the highest appreciation in the year, and one of the reasons is the greater exposure to the shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (14.46%).

First Argument - Momentum

When we analyze the momentum pragmatically, we see that there is a great opportunity. For reference, the chart below shows that the iShares MSCI World ETF outperforms the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF over the last 10 years.

However, when we look at the last 25 years (before the launch of ETFs), MSCI Emerging Markets outperforms MSCI World. Therefore, in a long cycle, emerging markets are still more promising given their history.

The most curious thing is that the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF is outperforming the iShares MSCI World ETF this year; in other words, we may be seeing the beginning of a new cycle, with better performance from emerging markets in the coming years.

Second Argument - Discount

When we see the chart above, we might think that the emerging markets discount has reduced, but that is not true. According to the chart below, despite the recent rise, emerging markets remain extremely cheap.

As I follow a value investing philosophy, it is desirable to invest in markets with discounted prices; therefore, the chart above supports the buy recommendation.

Third Argument - Interest Rate Cuts

Finally, I bring again a table cited in another article, which shows the return of asset classes in cycles of interest cuts by the FED. As we can see below, the asset class that benefits most from this is emerging markets.

In my opinion, there is a good possibility that interest rate cuts will continue in 2026, even according to President Trump's indications, and consequently emerging markets will become more attractive.

Fourth Argument - Valuation

To close the arguments that make me confident with this thesis, let's talk about valuation. The possibility of exposure to artificial intelligence combined with diversification into emerging markets is an interesting idea in my opinion.

However, it is difficult to find an investment with exposure to artificial intelligence where the multiple is not at an all-time high, but this is not the case. As we can see below, in the last 5 years the EEM ETF has traded at 18x ​​earnings, and currently trades at 14x earnings.

Therefore, if the ETF returns to trading at 18x ​​earnings, there is an upside of 28% that supports my buy recommendation. Additionally, the trigger has already been triggered by momentum, in my opinion. I am confident in the recommendation because Seeking Alpha's Quant recommendations also indicate buy.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

There are several risks to the investment thesis. The first is that emerging countries tend to have volatile currencies, and when we add this to democracies, there is a great alternation of power. As a result, several regulatory changes may occur due to the alternation of governments, and this may affect the scenario for many companies in this ETF.

When we think about the macroeconomic scenario, the economies of emerging countries tend to be very dependent on commodities; therefore, there is an economy that is cyclical in nature. Additionally, there are several examples, such as Brazil, that have a fragile fiscal situation. Therefore, there are several additional challenges for preferring to invest in emerging markets over developed markets.

The Bottom Line

As we saw in this article, the idea of ​​investing in emerging markets was very successful in 2025, but what about in 2026? In my opinion, the scenario remains attractive, as I was able to show through the four arguments presented in this article.

The momentum of assets remains attractive; at the same time, the discount for developed markets attracts positive attention. Additionally, the Fed should continue cutting interest rates, and valuation remains attractive in my opinion, especially due to its exposure to artificial intelligence.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF. In my opinion, this is not an obvious thesis, and the risk-return relationship is very interesting.

data.title: BITU: Wait For $73,000 Bitcoin (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Just two months after seemingly breaking out to a new all-time high of $126,000 per coin, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has subsequently fallen all the way to $80,500. Following that late-November low, the coin has since rallied 17% after drawing down over 35% from the October peak. The million-dollar question; did we just see the lows, or is this just a relief rally before the coin makes a new low either later this year or in Q1-26. In this article, I'll focus entirely on my technical view of BTC and make the case for why I think traders should wait a bit longer before taking any shots at a product like the ProShares Ultra Bitcoin ETF (BITU).

BITU Details & Historical Performance

BITU is a fund that I've covered for Seeking Alpha a few times in the past. It is not one that I have ever called a 'buy' despite occasionally taking swing positions in shares. The fundamental problem with choosing something like BITU as a long-term investment is the fact that it's a 2x leveraged fund that rebalances daily. The ultimate goal is to double the returns of Bitcoin on a day-to-day basis. For instance, if Bitcoin increased by 3% in a single session, we would reasonably expect BITU to increase by 6%. This leverage works against BITU holders when Bitcoin is going down.

For instance, looking at the total return of BITU against a simple spot Bitcoin ETF since the peak in October says it all; if you've been holding this fund through this decline, you've been getting absolutely wrecked. It bears repeating that these funds are not designed for long-term holding. In fact, when I've held 2x leverage funds, I don't generally like to keep them for longer than a few days unless I'm completely convinced I have a sustained directional move correct. In which case, 2x leveraged funds work very well. But they're not good during times of chop or for long periods of time.

In my first article covering BITU back in May 2024, I shared a hypothetical 20-session period of chop to illustrate why holding BITU for longer than a couple of weeks would ultimately be detrimental to investment returns. Consider the performance of BITU relative to a spot fund since its inception in April 2024:

Bitcoin, while well-off highs, is still up almost 40%. Yet if you held BITU instead, you're actually down on your position even after the fund's sizable dividend payments. Still, in spite of the clear reasons against holding BITU, the fund can perform quite well when Bitcoin is in a sustained rally. Thus, what are traders to do today with Bitcoin battling for $90,000?

My Read on Bitcoin's Setup

I'll start by sharing my view of Bitcoin's monthly chart:

This should not be a chart that is unfamiliar to my followers, as I've shared it numerous times over the last several months. And I think the implications are pretty straightforward; I believe we've seen Bitcoin's cycle top already. Bitcoin recently breaking below its 18-month uptrend further validates that assumption. I know that there are many out there, even a few of my readers, who believe Bitcoin cycle theory is over. I don't hold that view. Frankly, I don't know how you could possibly think halving events matter going forward and simultaneously believe Bitcoin's boom cycles are over. But I digress.

Bitcoin fell 84% after the 2017 peak. It fell 77% after the 2021 peak. Even if we're generous and assume the coin falls by just 50% this time, that still implies another 30% of downside from current levels. And I suspect that level will align very well with a test of the 50-month moving average when it inevitably happens again. Regardless, on a more short-medium term time horizon, I think the asset will give us several opportunities to play relief bounces.

Shifting to the weekly chart, we can really get a sense for how many bearish RSI divergences it takes to find a price peak for BTC. In each of these last two boom cycles, I count no less than 3 clear instances of new price highs met with lower RSI highs. We also see the coin currently hanging on to the 100-week MA in red. The daily chart, though, is primarily why I think traders would be wise to pump the brakes on any leveraged long positions through something like BITU:

While this chart might look like a mess of lines, I like to utilize just a few things and keep it very simple; I draw diagonal trends and horizontal support/resistance levels. While Bitcoin's rally from $80k has been strong, I suspect it is running out of chances to break out above $94k. I believe the coin is going to pull back further, especially given the apparent weakness in tech and AI names that are starting to lose their darling status with the market. For a much stronger indication of a buyable short-term bottom, I'd like to see the kind of RSI divergences that we see all over the weekly chart. I don't think Bitcoin necessarily has to break $70k before we get another buyable dip, but I do think breaking $80k and generating a bullish RSI divergence (or two) would be a much more favorable entry point. Should we get such an opportunity, I think BITU would do very well.

Closing Summary

To reiterate, it is not advised that BITU be held for longer than a few days or weeks. Experienced traders who know how to manage risk can certainly make nice returns over short periods of time when they use 2x leveraged products at important pivot points. I suspect Bitcoin is at one of those pivot points again. However, I don't think playing a 2x swing position from the long side is the right play today. To be clear, I could certainly be wrong. I've been wrong many times before and will be again. But I was telling people to be cautious near the highs when many bulls didn't want to hear it. I wasn't wrong then, and I don't think I'm wrong now either. Time will tell. But I'm waiting for $73,000 BTC before I ramp up exposure or take swing positions through products like BITU.

data.title: Boeing: 777X Wins Big, Lower 737 MAX Deliveries Are No Surprise
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) is making steady progress improving its quality system and increasing production rates on key commercial programs such as the Boeing 737 MAX and Boeing 787. The acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems is one of the elements that should give Boeing better control over quality, targeting increasing but sustainable production rates to support an improving balance sheet. In this report, I discuss the airplane orders and deliveries for Boeing in November.

Boeing 777X And 787 Dreamliner Shine In Airplane Order Mix

For this report, I'm using a tool developed by The Aerospace Forum to visualize airplane orders, deliveries, cancellations, and associated values.

In November, Boeing booked 164 orders compared to 15 orders in the prior month. Orders consisted of 43 single-aisle airplanes and 121 wide-body airplanes valued at $22.8 billion:

  • China Airlines ordered seven Boeing 777-9 airplanes.
  • Emirates ordered 65 Boeing 777-9 airplanes.
  • Etihad Airways ordered six Boeing 787-10 airplanes.
  • Gulf Air ordered 15 Boeing 787-9 airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer ordered 45 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer ordered two Boeng 777Fs.
  • An unidentified customer ordered one Boeing 787-9.
  • The US Air Force ordered 15 Boeing 767-2C airplanes.
  • Uzbekistan Airways ordered eight Boeing 787-9 airplanes.

During the month, the following changes were made to the order book:

  • TUI Travel Aviation took over delivery slots for two Boeing 737 MAX airplanes from BOC Aviation.
  • Air Canada cancelled an order for four Boeing 787-10s.
  • Etihad Airways cancelled an order for seven Boeing 787-10s and 10 Boeing 777X airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer cancelled an order for 10 Boeing 777X airplanes.
  • Comair cancelled an order for five Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Aerolineas Argentinas and an unidentified customer cancelled an order for one Boeing 737 MAX each.
  • Korean Air selected GE as the engine supplier for 10 Boeing 787-10s already on order.
  • Qatar Airways selected GE as the engine supplier for 55 Boeing 787-10s and 75 Boeing 787-10s already on order.
  • An unidentified customer selected GE as the engine supplier for 28 Boeing 787-9s.
  • Xiamen Airlines was identified as the customer for one Boeing 737 MAX.
  • Aerotranscargo was identified as the customer for one Boeing 777F.

Order inflow in November was carried by the Boeing 777X and Boeing 787, marking a strong month for wide-body orders. We saw Emirates recommitting to the Boeing 777X while Etihad reduced its orders for the Boeing 777X. Boeing booked a total of 164 orders while cancelling 38 orders, bringing the net orders to 126 valued at $17 billion. In the same month last year, Boeing booked 35 net orders valued at $3.20 billion.

Year-to-date, Boeing booked 1,000 orders and 92 cancellations, bringing the net orders to 908 units valued at $101.7 billion. In the comparable period last year, Boeing booked 427 orders and 57 cancellations, bringing the net orders to 370, valued at $30.4 billion.

We continue to see that year-on-year orders have substantially increased against a modest increase in cancellations. I believe the order inflow is driven by delayed orders in prior years, as delivery schedules were uncertain, and fresh delays on the Boeing 777X program may have allowed Emirates to place a follow-up order for the Boeing 777X at favorable terms. With better visibility in production plans, airlines are now ordering more jets from Boeing again.

ASC 606 adjustments decreased by 26 units. This was driven by a decrease of 12 units on the Boeing 777 program, a decrease of two units on the Boeing 787 program, and a decrease of 10 units on the Boeing 737 program. During the month, orders that were already doubtful were indeed cancelled, leading to a reduction in the adjustment tally.

ASC 606 adjustments might be a vague term. Therefore, I have provided a description form prior analyses about what they are:

ASC 606 adjustments are corrections to the backlog for orders where a purchase contract exists, but additional criteria are not met. A decrease in ASC 606 adjustments can be achieved when the order, in fact, does get cancelled or the additional criteria are met.

Boeing now has a total of 597 orders in the ASC 606 adjustment category, which covers 9.0% of the backlog. As previously stated, it does not mean that 9% of the backlog is necessarily prone to cancellation, but for 9% of the backlog, additional criteria are not yet met. Occasionally, this leads to cancellations. The company now has a fully adjusted backlog of 6,019 airplanes.

Boeing Airplane Deliveries Fall As Inventory Deliveries Run Out

In November, Boeing delivered 44 airplanes, consisting of 32 single-aisle airplanes and 12 wide-body airplanes valued at $3.4 billion:

  • Boeing delivered 32 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Boeing delivered four Boeing 767s, consisting of two Boeing 767-2Cs used as a baseline for the tanker airplane and two freighters.
  • Boeing delivered two 777F airplanes.
  • Boeing delivered a total of six Boeing 787 airplanes, consisting of three -9 models and three -10 models.

Boeing deliveries dropped by nine units sequentially, driven by lower deliveries on the Boeing 737 MAX program. In prior months, I have been highlighting the higher delivery profile for the program. The reduction in deliveries does not change my view that Boeing is executing well on its longer-term production increase path for the Boeing 737 MAX. In prior months, Boeing was still leaning on deliveries from inventory. By the end of Q3, Boeing only had five airplanes in inventory, excluding the yet-to-be-certified Boeing 737 MAX 7 and MAX 10 productions. So, the tailwind from inventory deliveries simply is not there anymore since there barely are any airplanes left in inventory, and that led to a decline in the deliveries. We do note that the delivery numbers were below the production rate of 42 airplanes per month. However, November is a shorter production month, and that also affects the deliveries. Wide-body deliveries were largely stable, with the Boeing 787 program seeing one delivery less compared to the prior month.

In the same month last year, Boeing delivered 13 airplanes valued at $1.1 billion, as Boeing’s deliveries were heavily impacted by a strike in Seattle. Year-to-date, Boeing has delivered 537 airplanes valued at $40.6 billion compared to 318 deliveries valued at $23.3 billion a year ago. So, while Boeing’s deliveries fell sequentially, there still is substantial year-on-year growth, while delivery numbers are already exceeding last year’s full-year delivery total of 384 airplanes.

The book-to-bill ratio, an indicator of supply-demand health, was 3.7x in terms of orders and 6.8x in terms of value. The ratio for the month reflects substantial order inflow along with a favorable mix against the delivery volumes and delivery value. Year-to-date, the book-to-bill ratio in units is 1.9x and 2.7x.

A Word On The Spirit AeroSystems Acquisition

On the 8th of December, Boeing completed the acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems. For Boeing, the acquisition is a big deal in the sense that it now has better control over the Boeing 737 MAX supply chain. Boeing divested Spirit AeroSystems 20 years ago, allowing Spirit to pursue supply work for other manufacturers, but eventually it also became a way for Boeing to offload risk to through its Partnering for Success program. Particularly after the incident with the Boeing 737 MAX 9 in January 2024 it became clear that the way the quality and supply chain system were functioning created substantial friction between Spirit and Boeing that did not benefit quality. In the aftermath of the incident, Boeing’s production rates were capped on the Boeing 737 MAX program, and for Spirit, that meant that the company was also facing substantial financial strain. For Boeing, to improve the supply chain health and quality, it became necessary to acquire Spirit AeroSystems. Prior to the Boeing 737 MAX crisis, Spirit AeroSystems generated around $500 million in free cash flow largely derived from the Boeing 737 program. While the governing motive for Boeing to acquire Spirit AeroSystems is not a financial one, ultimately the acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems can result in some cash flow tailwind.

Conclusion: Boeing Clears Additional Milestones

Boeing’s November order inflow showed strength driven by orders for the Boeing 777X and Boeing 737 MAX. In some sense, orders are not concerning at all. Boeing has a large backlog that will take years to deliver, and the key right now is to increase production rates in a sustainable way. The reduction in deliveries in November seemingly does not fit that frame. However, we do note that Boeing has little to no Boeing 737 MAX airplanes left to deliver from inventory, which is one step in the physical inventory and balance sheet cleaning process. It is one of several steps that Boeing is executing to run a leaner balance sheet. While operational strength improves, we should be able to see Boeing’s cash flow strength once again. First, that cash flow strength will be used to deleverage, and after that it can be used to partially fund new airplane developments and consider shareholder returns. The stark contrast is that cash flow is no longer the centerpiece of Boeing’s decision-making. The quality of production is and cash flow is the distributable byproduct.

data.title: DNOW: Improving Fundamentals Turn This Cheap Stock Into A Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Thesis

I maintained a neutral stance when I last covered the DNOW Inc. (DNOW) stock earlier this year due to a weak growth outlook with lingering industry-related headwinds from OPEC policy shifts and tariff uncertainty. As a result, the stock has been down in the single-digit percentage, significantly underperforming the market. However, the company showed a slight recovery in the last quarter, which, along with solid growth in the U.S. segment, helped it deliver its strongest revenue quarter in the last five years in its latest Q3 2025 results.

While the demand environment remains strong, primarily across the U.S. process solutions business, and broader market activity in other areas continues to improve with capital investment from customers, I expect the company's topline to continue to grow steadily over the coming quarters. Acquisition of MRC Global has also expanded the company's reach meaningfully across the vertical, which should further support the company's topline growth beyond FY25.

With margins expected to stay strong with volume growth and disciplined cost management, the company's overall growth trajectory looks favorable going forward. Therefore, as a discounted valuation, I believe the stock finally warrants an upgrade to a decent buy for investors with a long-term view.

DNOW’s Q3 25 Highlights

Although DNOW continues to experience softness across its Canada and International segments moving into the second half of 2025, it reported its Q3 results with decent single-digit growth due to continued strength in the core U.S. market. In Q3, DNOW’s consolidated topline grew approximately 4.6% year-on-year to a record $634 million, due to a strong 9.3% growth in the U.S. segment with healthy demand for its energy and industrial equipment across the region. However, revenue in the Canada and International segments was weak year over year, declining about 18.5% and 8.5%, respectively, during the third quarter of 2025.

Healthy overall topline growth also benefited the company’s profitability as its EBITDA margins expanded to 8%, about 110 bps higher than the same quarter a year ago. Operational leverage in the U.S. segment significantly benefited the margins during the quarter. EBITDA growth during the quarter also benefited the company’s bottom line performance, as its adjusted EPS increased to $0.26 from $0.21 a year ago, surpassing the consensus estimate by $0.03 during Q3 2025.

Outlook

In the past year, DNOW saw sluggish growth due to challenges from tariff uncertainty, customer consolidation, and muted upstream spending followed by OPEC+ policy shifts. However, the company exited Q3 with the highest revenue since 2020, showing signs of recovery as market activity improves in the key U.S. region.

While demand for natural gas remains strong with rising LNG exports, gas-fired power generation, and power needs for data centers, I expect the customer capital allocation to remain strong going forward. This should continue to support the demand for DNOW’s equipment in its key U.S. Energy Center business over the coming quarters.

On the other hand, the Process Solutions business of the company’s U.S. segment also continues to see solid traction, especially within its pump and water management products. In the past quarter, DNOW saw robust demand for its high-horsepower rental pump units and expects to secure multiple new orders for related offerings from chemical processing customers along the Gulf Coast, which should continue to benefit the segment revenue going ahead. Many customers are also increasingly giving priority to reliability and emission reduction, which is also likely to support demand for DNOW’s fabricated equipment, gas treatment technologies, and water-focused offerings further.

Apart from this, in November, DNOW completed its most-awaited acquisition of MRC Global. This transaction has notably increased the company's scale and expanded its customer base across the upstream, midstream, downstream, gas utility, and broader industrial sectors.

At last, talking about the company’s Canada and International segment, which collectively accounts for about 17% of total revenue as of Q3, broader market activity started to stabilize. While in Canada, the rig count remains 15% lower year over year, activity is improving sequentially with the rebound from seasonal headwinds, which should further support the segment growth as the company enters 2026. Meanwhile, the international business of DNOW is expected to remain a steady contributor with continued strength in the Middle East and Singapore and healthy demand for FPS conversions and LNG module fabrication.

Overall, while the Canada and International segments remain under pressure due to weaker activity as compared to 2024, their outlook appears to be improving with steady demand and sequential growth in broader market activity. Although I don’t expect these segments to meaningfully benefit the consolidated growth in the short term, they should remain steady contributors to the overall revenue. This, along with continued strength in the U.S. market driven by healthy demand for the company’s equipment, should support the company in sustaining its growth through the rest of FY25 and beyond.

Meanwhile, DNOW’s long-term outlook appears favorable as customers continue to allocate capital toward natural gas infrastructure, LNG expansion, and other grid-related projects, which is expected to continue to fuel long-term demand for the company’s offerings.

Additionally, the company also continues to invest to expand into adjacent markets like RNG, water management, and electrical solutions to diversify further. Strategic M&As also remain a key focus for the company to broaden its addressable market and add complementary products to its offerings. I think this, along with greater opportunities for growth in energy evolution and industrial markets with the company's recent MRC acquisition, further stands to benefit its consolidated topline growth over the coming years.

Valuation

I previously covered the DNOW stock earlier this year in March, maintaining my neutral stance despite its cheap valuation, as growth prospects appeared unfavorable in the short term. Since then, the company's growth across the top and bottom lines has been nearly flat. As a result, the stock continues to trade sideways and is currently down by about 4% since my hold rating, significantly underperforming the S&P 500, which grew over 22% during the same period.

Currently, the DNOW stock is trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E of 16.97, based on its FY25 earnings estimate of $0.84. The stock remains cheap with a notable discount to its sector and five-year forward average multiple. However, this time the stock looks slightly attractive as well, given the improving activity across most of the regions and strength in the core U.S. market.

Going ahead, the company's consolidated topline looks well-positioned to sustain growth over the coming quarters, as demand across the company's process solution and energy center business remains healthy and recovery continues across other areas. While topline is anticipated to continue to grow steadily, the company's focus on disciplined cost management and benefit from operational leverage on higher sales should continue to support margin growth in FY25 and beyond.

The company's balance sheet also remains clean with zero debt. Considering this, gains from higher volumes and strong margins should largely translate into bottom-line growth, further enhancing the stock valuation in the future.

Risks

A key risk for DNOW remains its exposure to cyclicality in energy-related capital spending. And any pullback in customer spending due to reduced commodity prices or a change in project timeline could significantly weigh on the company's short-term growth.

Apart from this, while the company's Canada and international businesses are experiencing sequential improvement, they continue to face uncertainty from weaker activity levels compared to 2024. Although I expect this to continue to improve going forward, sustained weakness versus the prior year could continue to weigh on the company's year-on-year growth in the short term.

Overall, in my view, while the stock valuation remains attractive, it may continue to underperform if growth fails to accelerate meaningfully or if it can't deliver as expected in its upcoming earnings.

Final Verdict And Rating Upgrade

As discussed above, the DNOW stock remains cheap after a prolonged underperformance and is trading below its historical averages. However, market activity appears to improve across the region, which, along with a strong demand environment, particularly in the U.S. market, and a focus on cost reduction, should benefit the company's topline and margins over the coming quarters. Given an improving outlook, greater scale from the addition of MRC Global, and the company's continued focus on diversification, I believe the turnaround may be around the corner. Therefore, while the valuation remains attractive, I am upgrading the DNOW stock to a BUY for long-term investors.

data.title: Despite Price Drop, ONE Gas Is Not A Bargain Yet
data.description:
The utility sector has not fared well over the last couple of months. The State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) is currently trading over 9% off its high for the year that was reached in October. The Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight Utilities ETF (RSPU) has fallen about the same amount during the last two months, which shows the drop is much more sector-based than it is related to any small number of companies.

Earlier this week, I wrote about the decline in price for Southern Company (SO). Its stock has lost about 15% of its value in the last two months. I rated the stock as a Buy as I think the market has overreacted to some concerns for that company. I have been looking through the sector to see what other buying opportunities may exist heading into the end of 2025.

ONE Gas (OGS) has followed a similar pattern to its sector recently, although its descent has been more recent. OGS has lost about 9.2% of its value since the end of November. After a review of the company’s operations and recent financial results, I think this pullback in price may be more warranted than it was in the case of SO. Even though ONE Gas is approaching a double-digit decline in the last three weeks, its stock is still up over 11% year-to-date. I recommend a “wait and see” approach to OGS heading into the new year.

Company Overview

ONE Gas is a holding company for three regulated natural gas utilities – Kansas Gas Service, Oklahoma Natural Gas Company, and Texas Gas Service. Through its subsidiaries, the company serves over 2.3 million customers that include residential, commercial, industrial, and wholesale buyers of natural gas. Headquartered in Tulsa, the company was founded in 1906 and has roughly 3900 employees. A more complete breakdown of the business operations for the three state utilities is given below.

OGS has a market cap of $4.6 billion. Its revenues in the first nine months of 2025 have totaled $1.74 billion, which represents a 19.6% increase from the same time period in 2024. Almost all of that increase can be attributed to natural gas sales that increased almost $290 million during the first three quarters, year-over-year. Gas sales make up 90.1% of the company’s top line number. Gas transportation revenues are the next highest revenue category, totaling $105.4 million, or 6.1% of the revenue for ONE Gas.

Regulated utilities tend to carry a high amount of debt, and OGS is no different. However, its current borrowing position appears to be quite manageable with no long-term debt due until 2029. Interest expense on the company’s last income statement actually decreased from $107.5 million in the first three quarters of 2024 to just $106.3 million in the same period this year. In October of this year, ONE Gas amended the terms of its short-term credit facility to increase its capacity to $1.5 billion from the previous level of $1.35 billion. The term of the agreement now extends through October of 2030. As of September 30, the company had solid, investment-grade credit ratings of A3 and A- from Moody’s and S&P, respectively. A full description of the company's long-term debts is listed below.

ONE Gas paid out a quarterly dividend of $0.67 this year, with the last payment being made at the start of this month. Its stock currently yields 3.49%, just 0.02% below the median for the utility sector. The company has elected to increase its payout annually for 11 consecutive years, and it has already implied that the streak will continue next year. The increase is typically announced in mid-January.

Analysis of ONE Gas

ONE Gas released its most recent quarterly results on November 3. The company reported earnings of $0.44 per diluted share, $0.02 ahead of analysts’ estimates and $0.10 better than Q3 2024. Year-to-date, the company has netted $2.94 per share, compared to $2.56 per share in the same period in 2024. As part of the report, OGS narrowed its guidance for FY 2025, with its most recent expectations calling for net income in the range of $262 million to $266 million, which would equate to earnings of $4.34 to $4.40 per share.

As part of a gas utilities conference hosted by Jeffries on December 11, ONE Gas gave the following guidance for FY 2026:

  • Net income of $294 million to $302 million
  • Earnings range of $4.65 to $4.77 per diluted share based on 63.4 million diluted shares outstanding
  • CapEx expected to be approximately $800 million. Of that total, about 39% will be spent in Texas and Oklahoma. The remaining 22% will be allocated to projects in Kansas.

Over the next five years, the company expects annual rate base growth of 7% to 9%. Total capital investments should total around $4.3 billion. Earnings per share are projected to grow 5% to 7%, an increase over previous estimates of 4% to 6%. The ratio of cash flow from operations to debt should rise to 20% by 2030, an improvement of 1% from 2026.

While some of these numbers, including the potential for earnings growth of about 7.8% for next year, look attractive, OGS is not really a bargain at its current price. Its current earnings multiple of 17.6 is slightly above its average from the last three years and just below its 10-year mean. Compared to the entire utilities sector, OGS is trading at a slight discount to the average. A graph of the stock's historical PE ratio is shown below.

Future earnings growth on a per-share basis will also be more challenging going forward, as the company has diluted shareholders by issuing more stock in recent years. Below are the common shares listed in the 10-K filings from the end of the following years:

  • 2022 - 55,350,277
  • 2023 - 56,546,000 (+2.2%)
  • 2024 - 59,877,552 (+5.9%)

For its earnings projections for next year, the company did reference 63.4 million diluted shares for its calculation, which could signal more new common shares will be issued in 2026.

Peer Comparison

When I summed up all of my findings for ONE Gas, I was neither particularly impressed nor discouraged. The company seemed to be rather typical in most of its metrics. For further clarity on my opinion of the company, I decided to conduct a peer comparison using four other regulated gas utilities. The four companies I chose were as follows:

  • Atmos Energy Corporation (ATO)
  • Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN)
  • Southwest Gas Holdings (SWX)
  • Spire Inc. (SR)

With its home base in Dallas, TX, Atmos Energy Corporation is the 800-pound gorilla in the natural gas space with a market cap of $27 billion, greater than the market caps for the other four companies on this list combined. NWN has operations in Texas in addition to Oregon and Washington State. The company also has some water services in addition to its natural gas operations. Southwest operates west of OGS in Arizona, Nevada, and California. Spire has regulated gas operations in Missouri, Alabama, and Mississippi.

In most of the metrics listed below, ONE Gas finds itself square in the middle of the pack. Its price-to-earnings and price-to-book ratios are just below the averages for this group. Its PEG ratio is slightly above the average. The company’s dividend yield and growth are ranked third from this group of five.

In short, there is nothing compelling that makes me want to reach for ONE Gas above any others listed here. The company seems to be valued in line with its peers, and I do not see any catalyst for growth in the year ahead that would be a differentiator for the company.

Risks to Consider

Regulated utilities are always exposed to the risk that any proposed rate increases will not be approved by state regulators. Adverse rate decisions can impact the company’s ability to recover its costs and pay its debt obligations. Political pressure is rising across the country as home energy prices are increasing, putting added pressure on the company to keep costs down and putting regulatory decisions in more of a spotlight.

The stock price for ONE Gas can be affected by rising and falling interest rates, and OGS could face significant headwinds if interest rates were to rise in 2026. Utility stocks are often proxies for other fixed income investment options, but buying stocks involves risk, unlike CDs and money market accounts. When interest rates for those options increase, demand for utility stocks will likely fall as their fixed dividends become less attractive.

ONE Gas has a moderately high level of short interest, standing at 8.27% at the time I am writing this analysis. This shows a level of pessimism in the stock that is significantly higher than any of the peers listed above. The closest is Spire with 4.15%.

The Bottom Line

ONE Gas is currently a Hold according to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating scale. OGS has actually traded as a Hold for the last 12 months, as shown by the graph below. It should be noted that out of 13 natural gas utilities rated by Seeking Alpha, none of them have a Buy rating. Nine are rated as a Hold. Four are given Sell scores.

I think that OGS is likely to trade in line with the broader utility sector for the next few months. My evaluation of OGS is that the stock looks OK, but not great. If the price were to drop another 9% before the end of this year, it could be a more attractive option for 2026.
